did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), specifically DenseNet201, ResNet152V2, Se-ResNet152, Xception, InceptionV3, and VGG19, along with transfer learning and a novel ensemble model (DIX), to classify melanoma skin lesions as benign or malignant using the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from three distinct experiments: training six individual CNNs, applying transfer learning to these models, and proposing a novel ensemble model (DIX). It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 98.58% accuracy for DIX) on the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset and concludes with findings from these new computations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A study on Deep Convolutional Neural Networks, Transfer Learning, and Ensemble Model for Melanoma Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses melanoma detection by classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant using the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset from Kaggle. The researchers evaluated six individual Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet201, ResNet152V2, Se-ResNet152, Xception, InceptionV3, and VGG19) and applied transfer learning techniques. The highest accuracy achieved by individual models was 89%, while transfer learning with Se-ResNet152 yielded 86%. To improve performance, a novel rank-based ensemble model named DIX (combining DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and Xception) was proposed. The DIX ensemble model outperformed all other methods, achieving a superior accuracy of 98.58%. The results confirm that ensemble models provide more reliable and accurate solutions for melanoma cancer detection compared to single CNNs or transfer learning approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset used for classification; 7,289 images originally, augmented to 12,000.
One of the two classes in the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset used for classification; 6,611 images originally, augmented to 12,000.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs Malignant melanoma skin lesions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between benign and malignant classes using the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) with reported accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset collected from Kaggle containing high-quality dermatological images of melanoma skin lesions. Images were captured using a microscope and stored as PNG files in RGB format.

use.training: Used for training six individual CNNs (DenseNet201, ResNet152V2, Se-ResNet152, Xception, InceptionV3, VGG19) and transfer learning models. The dataset was augmented to 12,000 images per class (total 24,000) and split into training, validation, and testing subsets using a 70%, 15%, and 15% stratified random sampling ratio.
use.validation: Used for validation (15% of the augmented dataset) to ensure balanced representation of classes during model development.
use.testing: Used for testing (15% of the augmented dataset) to evaluate the performance of the models, including the proposed DIX ensemble model.
use.other: The original dataset consisted of 13,900 images (7,289 benign, 6,611 malignant) resized to 224x224 pixels. Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zoom, displacement, brightness/contrast changes) was applied to balance the classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on comparative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance tests.

Experiment 1: Original CNN Networks.notes: Xception and VGG19 achieved the highest accuracy of 89%. SeResNet152 had the lowest accuracy at 84%.

Experiment 2: Transfer Learning CNN Netw.notes: SeResNet152 achieved the highest accuracy of 86% among transfer learning models. Transfer learning generally yielded lower accuracy compared to original CNN architectures.

Experiment 3: Ensemble Model (DIX).notes: The DIX ensemble model outperformed all individual CNN and transfer learning models. It correctly classified 1811 malignant (TP) and 2020 benign (TP) samples. The model achieved high accuracy with minimal overfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: Six individual Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet201, ResNet152V2, Se-ResNet152, Xception, InceptionV3, VGG19) and a novel rank-based ensemble model named DIX (combining DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and Xception).
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights were utilized for the CNN models. Transfer learning was applied in the second experiment, though it yielded lower accuracy than original CNN training on this specific dataset.

ensemble_model.name: DIX
ensemble_model.integration_method: Rank-based ensemble using weighted average of probability scores from individual models. Probability values from each model are summed for every class, and the class with the highest normalized sum is selected as the final prediction.

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, flipping, zoom, displacement, brightness/contrast changes. Dataset augmented to 12,000 images per class (total 24,000).

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Class with the highest probability (or highest normalized sum of probabilities in the ensemble) is selected.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: DIX (ensemble model combining DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and Xception)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization: Pixel values rescaled from 0-255 to 0-1.
Artifact/Hair Removal: Removal of hair strands and ruler marks using morphological operations and inpainting methods.
Contrast Enhancement: Application of CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) with clip limits of 2.0 and tile grid size of 8x8.
Color Normalization: Standardization of RGB channels based on mean and standard deviation to minimize variation due to lighting.
Data Augmentation: Random rotations (0-360 degrees), horizontal and vertical flipping, zoom (0.8 to 1.2), width and height displacement (up to 20), and brightness/contrast changes.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotations applied in the range of 0 to 360 degrees.
Horizontal and vertical flipping of images.
Zoom applied within the range of 0.8 to 1.2.
Width and height displacement of up to 20 pixels.
Changes to brightness and contrast levels.
Data augmentation was used to balance the classes, increasing the benign class from 7,289 to 12,000 images and the malignant class from 6,611 to 12,000 images.

conclusions: Among individual pre-trained Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), Xception and VGG19 achieved the highest accuracy of 89% for melanoma detection.

Transfer learning yielded lower accuracy compared to training original CNN architectures on this specific large, domain-specific dataset, with Se-ResNet152 achieving the highest transfer learning accuracy of 86%.
The proposed rank-based ensemble model DIX (combining DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and Xception) outperformed all individual CNN and transfer learning models, achieving a superior accuracy of 98.58%.
Ensemble models provide more reliable and accurate solutions for melanoma cancer detection compared to single CNNs or transfer learning approaches.
The study acknowledges that transfer learning does not always offer significant advantages when dealing with large, domain-specific datasets.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on a single, publicly available Kaggle dataset (Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.
Data augmentation was used to artificially balance classes, increasing the malignant class from 6,611 to 12,000 images, which may introduce synthetic bias and overestimate real-world performance.

Transfer learning yielded lower accuracy (max 86%) compared to training original CNN architectures from scratch (max 89%), suggesting pre-trained weights may not be optimal for this specific domain-specific dataset.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the superiority of the ensemble model over individual CNNs.
Critical hyperparameters including optimizer, loss function, learning rate, and number of epochs are not explicitly reported, hindering reproducibility.
The dataset contains images captured via microscope, which may not reflect the diversity of clinical dermoscopic or macroscopic imaging conditions found in real-world practice.
No discussion of potential data leakage risks or rigorous cross-validation strategies beyond a single stratified split (70/15/15).
